headline: Le Pen Keeps Ballot, Takes the Bracelet
deck: A Paris appeals court upheld Marine Le Pen's conviction for embezzling European Union funds while shortening her disqualification and ordering a year under an electronic ankle tag. On paper she remains eligible for the 2027 presidential race; in practice the bracelet may decide whether she or Jordan Bardella carries the National Rally flag.

art.title: The Bracelet Ballot
art.caption: Paris handed down the appeals verdict that leaves Le Pen eligible on paper while ordering a year under an electronic tag; Lyon and Marseille mark the national stages where the 2027 race may pivot. · Map: Tinkerton, Policy Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: Marine Le Pen left a Paris appeals court on 7 July carrying both an upheld conviction and a path to the 2027 presidential ballot that her lawyers had fought to preserve [E1]. The chamber confirmed her guilt in embezzling European Union funds through fictitious parliamentary assistant contracts at the National Rally, the verdict that had threatened to end her political career at the highest level [E1]. Yet the judges did not strip her eligibility outright; they shortened her ban from elected office and substituted a year of confinement under an electronic ankle monitor for part of a three-year prison term [E1][E3].

The sentence arithmetic rewards close reading. Le Pen received three years in prison with one to be served at home under the tag, while forty-five months of ineligibility came with thirty suspended, a bracket of mercy that leaves her technically able to stand in 2027 if the calendar holds [E3]. The appeals bench shortened the ban from elected office in a move that preserves her candidacy on paper while keeping a criminal record intact [E1][E3]. Whether that balance reflects standard appellate tailoring or something more deliberate remains the argument the case will feed for years [E3].

Logistics may matter as much as the statute. A presidential campaign demands rallies, travel, and the choreography of a national leader in perpetual motion, capacities that a year tethered to an electronic bracelet would compress severely [E2]. Le Pen has herself signaled she could not run while tagged, treating the monitor as a condition she rejects even as the court imposed it [E2]. Her party must therefore weigh whether to advance a convicted front-runner who refuses the very sanction that makes her nominally free to declare, or to recalibrate before the race hardens [E2].

French media moved within hours toward the succession scenario that National Rally has long kept in reserve. Party president Jordan Bardella has been positioned to run in 2027 if Le Pen cannot, a standby plan now activated by a ruling that leaves her legal status and personal willingness in tension [E5]. Bardella's candidacy would transfer the movement's electoral weight to a younger face unburdened by conviction, though whether the base would accept a substitute in place of its historic leader is untested ground [E5]. The Rassemblement National faces a choice between loyalty to Le Pen and the pragmatism of a cleaner standard-bearer [E5].

Opponents read the outcome without the ambiguity the arithmetic invites. Socialist spokesman Boris Vallaud told France's parliamentary channel that Le Pen is plainly “c'est une délinquante… coupable de détournement de fonds publics,” framing her as a delinquent guilty of diverting public funds and dismissing any narrative of judicial martyrdom [E4]. That blunt verdict from the left treats the conviction as the story's moral center and rejects the idea that shortened disqualification softens the underlying fraud [E4]. For Vallaud and his allies, the bracelet does not rehabilitate; it marks a criminal who should never again claim the republic's highest office [E4].

Two readings of the sentence coexist without easy resolution. One treats the appeals court as an independent institution doing what French courts routinely do: adjusting penalties within the law, suspending portions of bans that trial judges set too harshly, and substituting house arrest for carceral time where statutes allow [E1][E3]. The other sees a political system solving a political problem through sentencing design, producing an outcome that punishes Le Pen without gifting her a ban that would rally nationalist sympathy or installing a disqualification so broad it would prejudge an election [E1][E2]. The null hypothesis matters: suspended months and electronic monitoring are standard French practice, not theatrical choreography invented for one defendant [E3].

A Cassation appeal still looms, and France's highest court has said it would aim to rule before the 2027 election, leaving months in which legal and campaign clocks may diverge [E2]. Until then the verdict belongs to nobody fully: the conviction stands, the candidacy survives on paper, and the front-runner who might yet claim the Élysée remains free to declare and tethered to the ankle the court ordered [E1][E2]. The bracelet is the punch the arithmetic hides in plain sight, a year of visible sanction that may accomplish what a disqualification ban would have done in the open [E2]. She keeps the ballot; the tag may take the campaign.
